section.title: Ministry Begins in Galilee
section.sectionRationale: With John arrested, Jesus steps forward with His opening announcement, the kingdom of God is near, and begins to gather a people. He calls fishermen, teaches with startling authority, drives out an unclean spirit, heals Peter's mother-in-law and a whole town, withdraws to pray, and cleanses a leper. In one packed day and its aftermath Mark shows the arrival of the kingdom in word and power.
section.thesis: The nearness of God's reign in Jesus is proven by His authoritative word: it summons disciples, silences demons, heals the sick, and cleanses the outcast, so that the only fitting response is to repent and believe the good news.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the kingdom announced; the first disciples called; a day of authority in Capernaum; prayer and a wider mission; and the cleansing of a leper.

units.label: The kingdom announced
units.note: repent and believe the good news

verses.text: After the arrest of John, Jesus went into Galilee and proclaimed the {{euangelion|gospel}} of God.
“The time is fulfilled,” He said, “and the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God is near. Repent and {{pisteuo|believe}} in the gospel!”
As Jesus was walking beside the Sea of Galilee, He saw Simon and his brother Andrew. They were casting a net into the sea, for they were fishermen.
“Come, follow Me,” Jesus said, “and I will make you fishers of men.”
And at once they left their nets and followed Him.
Going on a little farther, He saw James son of Zebedee and his brother John. They were in a boat, mending their nets.
Immediately Jesus called them, and they left their father Zebedee in the boat with the hired men and followed Him.

units.label: A day of authority in Capernaum
units.note: even the unclean spirits obey Him

verses.text: Then Jesus and His companions went to Capernaum, and right away Jesus entered the synagogue on the Sabbath and began to teach.
The people were astonished at His teaching, because He taught as one who had {{exousia|authority}}, and not as the scribes.
Suddenly a man with an unclean spirit cried out in the synagogue:
“What do You want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have You come to destroy us? I know who You are, the Holy One of God!”
But Jesus rebuked the spirit. “Be silent!” He said. “Come out of him!”
At this, the unclean spirit threw the man into convulsions and came out with a loud shriek.
All the people were amazed and began to ask one another, “What is this? A new teaching with authority! He commands even the unclean spirits, and they obey Him!”
And the news about Jesus spread quickly through the whole region of Galilee.
As soon as Jesus and His companions had left the synagogue, they went with James and John to the home of Simon and Andrew.
Simon’s mother-in-law was sick in bed with a fever, and they promptly told Jesus about her.
So He went to her, took her by the hand, and helped her up. The fever left her, and she began to serve them.
That evening, after sunset, people brought to Jesus all the sick and demon-possessed,
and the whole town gathered at the door.
And He healed many who were ill with various diseases and drove out many demons. But He would not allow the demons to speak, because they knew who He was.

units.label: Prayer and a wider mission
units.note: that is why I have come

verses.text: Early in the morning, while it was still dark, Jesus got up and went out to a solitary place to pray.
Simon and his companions went to look for Him,
and when they found Him, they said, “Everyone is looking for You!”
But Jesus answered, “Let us go on to the neighboring towns so I can preach there as well, for that is why I have come.”
So He went throughout Galilee, preaching in their synagogues and driving out demons.
Then a leper came to Jesus, begging on his knees: “If You are willing, You can make me {{katharizo|clean}}.”
Moved with {{splanchnizomai|compassion}}, Jesus reached out His hand and touched the man. “I am willing,” He said. “Be clean!”
And immediately the leprosy left him, and the man was cleansed.
Jesus promptly sent him away with a stern warning:
“See that you don’t tell anyone. But go, show yourself to the priest and present the offering Moses prescribed for your cleansing, as a testimony to them.”

But the man went out and openly began to proclaim and spread the news. Consequently, Jesus could no longer enter a town in plain view, but He stayed out in solitary places. Yet people came to Him from every quarter.

euangelion.gloss: Good news, glad tidings. Jesus proclaims the good news of God: that the long-awaited reign of God has arrived in Him. It is an announcement to be believed and obeyed, calling for repentance and trust.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, reign, kingly rule. The kingdom of God is God's saving royal power breaking into history in the person and work of Jesus. It is near because the King has come; it advances as His word heals, delivers, and gathers a people.

pisteuo.gloss: To believe, trust, rely on. Jesus pairs it with repentance as the fitting response to the good news: not mere agreement but a wholehearted turning to God and resting in His promise in Christ.

exousia.gloss: Authority, right, power to act. The crowds marvel that Jesus teaches with authority unlike the scribes, and that even unclean spirits obey Him. His authority is inherent, not borrowed, the authority of God's own King.

katharizo.gloss: To cleanse, make clean, purify. Leprosy made a person ceremonially unclean and cut off from the community. Jesus does not become unclean by touching the leper; instead His touch makes the unclean clean, a sign of the deeper cleansing He brings.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion, from the word for the inward parts; a gut-deep mercy. Jesus is stirred to the depths at the leper's plight and reaches out to touch the untouchable, showing the heart of God toward the outcast.

basic.context: John has been arrested, and Jesus steps into the open with His first sermon: the time has come, God's kingdom is near, so repent and believe. He calls four fishermen who drop everything to follow. In Capernaum He teaches with an authority that stuns everyone and casts out an unclean spirit. He heals Peter's mother-in-law and then a whole town at the door. Before dawn He slips away to pray, then presses on to preach elsewhere. Finally He touches and cleanses a leper, the untouchable outcast, moved with deep compassion.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as a display of what it means that the kingdom is near: the King's word carries authority over disciples, demons, disease, and defilement. Each scene answers the question of who Jesus is. Notice too the rhythm of power and prayer, and Jesus' insistence that His mission is to preach, not merely to draw crowds.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus preaches the euangelion, the good news, that the basileia, the kingdom of God, has drawn near, calling people to repent and pisteuo, believe. He teaches with exousia, authority, that astonishes. Moved with splanchnizomai, gut-deep compassion, He touches a leper and speaks katharizo, be clean.
basic.typology: Jesus cleansing the leper with a touch reverses the flow of defilement: under the law the clean became unclean by contact, but here the unclean is made clean. It anticipates the greater cleansing from sin He brings, and shows Him as the true priest who heals rather than merely diagnoses.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Holy One of God, as even the demon confesses. He is the King who announces the kingdom, the authoritative teacher, the stronger One who commands demons, the healer, and the compassionate cleanser of the outcast. His authority is His own, not borrowed.
basic.god: The good news is the good news of God: His reign, His initiative, His rescue. God draws near in Jesus with power and compassion, healing bodies and confronting evil, and calls people to turn to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 52 announces the good news of God's reign. Leviticus 13 and 14 give the law of the leper Jesus fulfils. Matthew 4 parallels the calling of the disciples.
basic.oneStory: The kingdom promised through the prophets arrives in Jesus. He gathers the first of a new people, defeats the powers that oppress, heals the broken, and cleanses the outcast, all signs that God's saving reign has broken in. The right response then and now is the same: repent and believe the good news.

context.literary: Mark compresses the opening of Jesus' ministry into rapid scenes joined by his favourite word, immediately. Verses 14 and 15 give the thesis of Jesus' preaching; the rest illustrates it. Capernaum's single Sabbath day, teaching, exorcism, healing, and a town at the door, functions as a representative day, followed by prayer, wider mission, and the leper.
context.historical: Galilee was a region of fishing villages and synagogues. Rabbis normally gathered disciples who applied to them; Jesus reverses this by summoning fishermen directly. Leprosy, a term covering various skin diseases, brought ceremonial uncleanness and social exclusion under Leviticus 13, so a leper approaching Jesus was a bold and desperate act.
context.canonical: Jesus' announcement fulfils the prophetic hope of God's reign, Isaiah 52:7. His command to the cleansed leper to show himself to the priest keeps the Mosaic law, Leviticus 14, while pointing beyond it to the One who cleanses at the root. The disciples' call begins the formation of the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summarize Jesus' core message, the nearness of God's kingdom calling for repentance and faith, and to demonstrate His unique authority over people, spirits, sickness, and uncleanness, so the reader grasps who the King is and how to respond.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe the King's authority in action; they are not a promise that every believer will heal on demand. The prescriptive core is Jesus' own summons in verse 15: repent and believe. The pattern of withdrawing to pray is a wise example, though not a rigid rule.

debates.question: Why does Jesus silence the demons and tell the leper to keep quiet, the so-called messianic secret?

views.label: To avoid a distorted view of Messiah
views.case: Popular hopes expected a political, conquering Messiah; premature acclaim would push a wrong agenda and hinder the path to the cross where His true kingship is revealed.

views.label: To refuse testimony from unclean sources and manage the crowds
views.case: Jesus will not have His identity announced by demons, and the swelling crowds already impede His preaching mission; the reticence protects the priority of the word over spectacle.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Jesus governs the timing and the meaning of His own revelation; His identity is finally made plain only at the cross, where a man, the centurion, confesses Him rightly.

debates.question: What does the immediate response of the fishermen teach about discipleship?

views.label: The call creates the response
views.case: Jesus' authoritative summons is itself powerful; they leave nets and father at once because His word effectually draws them, picturing the sovereign grace that calls disciples.

views.label: A model of wholehearted, costly following
views.case: Leaving livelihood and family shows that following Jesus reorders every loyalty; the scene sets the pattern of self-denial the Gospel will develop on the way to the cross.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: the King's call is powerful, and it summons a whole-life allegiance that leaves lesser loyalties behind.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus' opening proclamation gathers the Gospel's key terms: the euangelion, good news, of God, that the basileia, the kingdom or reign of God, has drawn near, met by repentance and pisteuo, faith. His teaching carries exousia, authority, an inherent right that the scribes lacked and that even unclean spirits must obey. At the leper's plea Jesus is moved with splanchnizomai, a compassion felt in the gut, and He speaks the word katharizo, be cleansed, making clean the very man the law shut out.

typology.type: The cleansing touch that makes the unclean clean
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true priest, who reverses defilement and brings the deeper cleansing from sin, Hebrews 7:26 and 1 John 1:7.
The reign of God foretold by the prophets, arriving in Jesus and consummated at His return, Isaiah 52:7 and Revelation 11:15.
The apostolic mission to gather people into God's kingdom, begun here and commissioned fully after the resurrection, Matthew 28:19.

study.christ: Jesus is the King who announces and embodies God's reign. He teaches with an authority all His own, and the unclean spirit, forced to speak truth, names Him the Holy One of God. He commands demons and they flee, He lifts the feverish and they are well, He touches the leper and defilement gives way to cleansing. Above all He is moved with the very compassion of God for the outcast, reaching out His hand to the untouchable. The authority and mercy displayed here will carry Him to the cross, where the Holy One is made unclean for us so that we might be cleansed.
study.god: The good news is expressly the good news of God, whose kingdom has drawn near. God takes the initiative to rescue: He sends His King to preach, to break the grip of evil spirits, to heal the sick, and to cleanse the outcast. God's reign is not distant or theoretical but arrives with power and tenderness, confronting everything that spoils His world and calling people home through repentance and faith.

crossReferences.note: How beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news, who proclaims that God reigns, fulfilled in Jesus' announcement of the kingdom.
The Mosaic ritual for cleansing a healed leper, to which Jesus sends the man as a testimony.
The parallel account of Jesus calling Simon, Andrew, James, and John from their nets.
Jesus appoints the Twelve to be with Him and to preach and cast out demons, continuing the mission begun here.

study.oneStory: The reign of God long promised by the prophets breaks into history in Jesus of Galilee. He preaches its nearness, calls the first of a renewed people, and proves the kingdom's arrival by overpowering demons, disease, and defilement. Every scene shows the King setting right what sin has spoiled, and points ahead to the cross where His authority and compassion meet, and to the day when the kingdom now near will fill the earth. The call that opened His ministry still stands: repent and believe the good news.

quiz.q: What is the heart of Jesus' opening proclamation in Galilee?

options.text: The time is fulfilled and God's kingdom is near, so repent and believe the good news.
options.note: Mark 1:15 gives Jesus' own summary message.

options.text: The kingdom of God is near, so get ready for it.
options.note: Right about the kingdom's nearness, but it omits the double call to repent and believe that Jesus attaches.

options.text: People must keep the law more carefully to earn God's favour.
options.note: Jesus calls for repentance and faith in response to grace arriving, not law-keeping to earn favour.

options.text: Israel should prepare to overthrow Rome.
options.note: Jesus announces the reign of God, not a political revolt.

quiz.q: How did the first disciples respond to Jesus' call?

options.text: They left their nets and their father at once and followed Him.
options.note: Mark 1:18 and 1:20 show their immediate, wholehearted response.

options.text: They followed Him but kept fishing part-time.
options.note: They did become fishers of men, but Mark stresses they left everything at once, not a part-time arrangement.

options.text: They asked for time to consider before deciding.
options.note: The text emphasizes immediacy; they did not delay to deliberate.

options.text: They refused until Jesus performed a miracle.
options.note: They followed at His word, before any miracle was demanded.

quiz.q: Why were the people astonished at Jesus in the Capernaum synagogue?

options.text: He taught with authority, unlike the scribes, and even unclean spirits obeyed His command.
options.note: Mark 1:22 and 1:27 tie their amazement to His authority in word and over demons.

options.text: He quoted the scribes and rabbis more skillfully than others.
options.note: The opposite: His authority was unlike the scribes, inherent rather than derived from citing others.

options.text: He performed clever tricks to entertain the crowd.
options.note: His works were real acts of authority and mercy, not entertainment.

options.text: He said nothing remarkable at all.
options.note: Mark says the crowd was amazed at His authoritative teaching.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' touching and cleansing of the leper reveal?

options.text: His deep compassion and His power to make the unclean clean rather than being defiled Himself.
options.note: Moved with compassion, Jesus touches the untouchable and cleanses him, Mark 1:41-42.

options.text: That Jesus cared about people's physical suffering.
options.note: True but partial; it also reveals His authority to reverse uncleanness and picture cleansing from sin.

options.text: That Jesus was willing to break the law of Moses.
options.note: Jesus actually upholds the law, sending the man to the priest per Leviticus 14; the deeper point is His cleansing power.

options.text: That leprosy was not really a serious condition.
options.note: Leprosy meant exclusion and defilement; Jesus' act is significant precisely because the condition was grave.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus leave the crowds to pray and then move on to other towns?

options.text: His mission was to preach the good news widely, so He would not be held by one town's demand for miracles.
options.note: Mark 1:38, that is why I have come, shows His priority on preaching beyond Capernaum.

options.text: He wanted time alone with the Father before more ministry.
options.note: True, He withdrew to pray, but His stated reason for moving on was to preach in other towns.

options.text: He was tired of the people of Capernaum.
options.note: It was mission priority, not weariness with the people, that moved Him on.

options.text: He had failed to help anyone in Capernaum.
options.note: He had healed many; He moved on to extend, not because of failure.

questions: Jesus' first word is repent and believe. What would genuine repentance and fresh trust look like in your life this week?
The fishermen left their nets at once. What might Jesus be asking you to leave or reorder in order to follow Him more fully?
Even the demons could not stand against Jesus' word. How does His authority over the powers of darkness give you confidence in your own struggles?
Jesus rose before dawn to pray, even with a town clamouring for Him. How is the rhythm of your life shaped by time alone with God?
Jesus was moved with compassion and touched the untouchable. Who are the outcasts near you that His compassion would have you reach toward?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts read the gospel of the kingdom of God; the shorter reading, the gospel of God, is well supported and means the same good news of God's reign.
A few manuscripts read moved with indignation rather than moved with compassion; the compassion reading is widely attested and fits the tender act of touching the leper.

The demon's words originally carried a dash in some printings; here the sense is rendered with a clean break, I know who You are, the Holy One of God, an unwilling but true confession of Jesus' identity.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 1:14-45. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 24 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
